graph.label: Fourier Analysis normalized knowledge graph
design_notes: Nodes represent mathematical items: definitions, structures, examples, counterexamples, theorems, proofs, proof steps, methods, and applications.
Edges are directed and typed; parallel edges are allowed when they encode distinct semantic/dependency relations.
The graph intentionally separates statement dependencies, proof dependencies, construction dependencies, and pedagogical prerequisites.
Trees, syllabi, proof maps, and application maps should be extracted as views, not treated as the underlying graph.
Fourier analysis is represented as a bridge from wave decompositions to Hilbert-space projection geometry, distribution theory, harmonic analysis, PDE, tomography, and signal processing.

domains.label: Foundations and language
domains.description: Global concepts, spaces, notation, and mental models.

domains.label: Kernels and summability
domains.description: Convolution kernels, approximate identities, and regularized summation.

domains.label: Convergence theory
domains.description: Modes of convergence, positive and negative results, localization.

domains.label: Hilbert, Lp, and operator viewpoint
domains.description: Projection geometry, orthonormal bases, and functional analysis context.

domains.label: Fourier transform on R and Rd
domains.description: Non-periodic frequency analysis on Euclidean space.

domains.label: Distributions and weak analysis
domains.description: Test functions, generalized functions, weak derivatives, and tempered distributions.

domains.label: Harmonic analysis
domains.description: Singular integrals, maximal functions, group Fourier analysis, and modern refinements.

domains.label: PDE and mathematical physics
domains.description: Heat, wave, Laplace, Poisson, Radon, and uncertainty applications.

domains.label: Finite Fourier analysis and signal processing
domains.description: DFT, FFT, sampling, spectra, filtering, and discrete convolution.

domains.label: Examples and counterexamples
domains.description: Canonical examples showing scope, failure modes, and necessity of hypotheses.

items.label: Fourier analysis
items.definition: The field that studies functions, signals, and operators by decomposing them into oscillatory building blocks such as characters, exponentials, sines, and cosines.
items.intuition: Replace a complicated object by its alignment with pure waves; geometry becomes frequency bookkeeping.

items.label: Frequency
items.definition: A parameter indexing oscillation rate; on the circle frequencies are integers, while on R^d frequencies are continuous vectors.
items.intuition: Frequency tells how many waves fit into space or time.

items.label: Complex exponential
items.definition: The basic oscillatory function that packages sine and cosine into one algebraic object.
items.intuition: A point rotating around the unit circle; Fourier analysis measures how much a function rotates with each speed.

items.label: Character
items.formal_statement: A character on an abelian group G is a homomorphism \chi:G\to \mathbb T.
items.definition: A multiplicative oscillation compatible with the group law.
items.intuition: The group-theoretic version of a pure frequency.

items.label: Integer frequency character
items.formal_statement: x\mapsto e^{inx},\quad n\in\mathbb Z,\ x\in\mathbb T.
items.definition: The standard characters of the circle group.
items.intuition: The integer-frequency waves wrapping around the circle.

items.label: Orthogonality
items.definition: A relation meaning two vectors or functions have zero inner product.
items.intuition: No alignment: projecting one onto the other gives nothing.

items.label: Superposition principle
items.statement: Linear combinations of solutions to a linear problem are again solutions.
items.intuition: Linear systems let pure waves be added without interacting.

items.label: Separation of variables
items.statement: Seek product-form solutions, reduce a PDE to ordinary differential equations, then superpose.
items.intuition: Split space-motion from time-motion; the equation forces allowed frequencies.

items.label: Orthogonal projection method
items.statement: Approximate a vector by its closest point in a finite-dimensional subspace using orthogonality.
items.intuition: Fourier partial sums are shadows of the function cast onto finitely many frequency directions.

items.label: Duality method
items.statement: Define or analyze an object by how it acts against test functions or linear functionals.
items.intuition: Instead of touching a rough object directly, measure it through smooth probes.

items.label: Circle group
items.formal_statement: \mathbb T=\mathbb R/(2\pi\mathbb Z) or \mathbb R/\mathbb Z depending on normalization.
items.definition: The compact abelian group of periodic phase.
items.intuition: A line whose ends are glued; integer frequencies fit exactly.

items.label: Periodic function
items.definition: A function repeating after a fixed period.
items.intuition: A signal whose pattern loops.
differentia: invariant under translation by the period

items.label: Fourier coefficient on the circle
items.definition: The scalar measuring the alignment of f with the frequency n.
items.intuition: A dot product with the nth pure wave.
differentia: computed by integration against a conjugate character

items.label: Fourier series
items.definition: The formal expansion of a periodic function into integer-frequency exponentials.
items.intuition: A periodic function decomposed into circular waves.
differentia: coefficients are Fourier coefficients of f

items.label: Trigonometric polynomial
items.definition: A finite linear combination of frequency characters.
items.intuition: A finite wave mixture.

items.label: Symmetric Fourier partial sum
items.definition: The degree-N trigonometric polynomial obtained by keeping frequencies |n|≤N.
items.intuition: The low-frequency approximation of f.

items.label: Sine and cosine series
items.definition: The real form of a Fourier series.
items.intuition: The same wave decomposition split into even and odd motion.

items.label: Even and odd extension
items.definition: A way to convert functions on an interval into periodic functions whose Fourier series are pure cosine or pure sine series.
items.intuition: Reflect the function symmetrically or antisymmetrically before wrapping it around the circle.

items.label: Uniqueness of Fourier coefficients
items.statement: Under suitable regularity, Fourier coefficients determine the function.
items.formal_statement: If f and g are continuous on the circle and \widehat f(n)=\widehat g(n) for all n\in\mathbb Z, then f=g.
items.intuition: All frequency measurements together pin down the signal.
hypotheses: f and g are continuous on the circle
all Fourier coefficients agree
items.conclusion: f=g

items.label: Convolution on the circle
items.definition: A translation-invariant weighted average of one function by another.
items.intuition: Slide one function across another and average the overlap.
differentia: integrates a shifted product over one period

items.label: Dirichlet kernel
items.definition: The kernel whose convolution with f gives the Nth Fourier partial sum.
items.intuition: The raw summation kernel; sharply oscillatory and not positive.

items.label: Good kernel
items.definition: A family of kernels whose mass is normalized, concentrated near zero, and controlled away from zero.
items.intuition: An approximate identity: it averages nearby values without letting distant oscillations dominate.

items.label: Approximate identity
items.definition: A net or sequence of kernels K_N such that f*K_N tends to f in an appropriate sense.
items.intuition: A blur that shrinks to a point while preserving total mass.

items.label: Fejer kernel
items.definition: The Cesaro-averaged Dirichlet kernel.
items.intuition: A nonnegative version of the summation kernel; averaging cancels harmful oscillation.

items.label: Cesaro mean of Fourier series
items.definition: The arithmetic average of Fourier partial sums.
items.intuition: Instead of trusting the last approximation, average all previous ones.

items.label: Fejer theorem
items.statement: Cesaro means of the Fourier series of a continuous periodic function converge uniformly to the function.
items.intuition: Positive local averaging recovers continuous functions uniformly.
hypotheses: f is continuous on the circle
items.conclusion: Cesaro means converge uniformly to f

items.label: Abel mean of Fourier series
items.definition: A radially damped Fourier series.
items.intuition: High frequencies are suppressed by a factor that vanishes geometrically.

items.label: Poisson kernel on the circle
items.definition: The kernel for Abel means and harmonic extension to the disk.
items.intuition: The boundary averaging kernel seen from radius r inside the disk.

items.label: Dirichlet kernels are not good kernels
items.statement: The family D_N fails the good-kernel criteria because of large oscillatory L^1 mass.
items.intuition: Raw partial sums can amplify oscillation rather than average it.

items.label: Good-kernel proof method
items.statement: Represent an approximant as f*K_N and prove convergence using concentration of K_N near zero plus continuity of f.
items.intuition: Split the integral into near and far parts: nearby values are close; far mass becomes negligible.

items.label: Mode of convergence
items.definition: The topology or sense in which approximants approach a function: pointwise, uniform, L^p, distributional, weak, or almost everywhere.
items.intuition: Fourier convergence questions are meaningless until the landing metric is specified.
differentia: specifies what it means to approach

items.label: Pointwise convergence
items.definition: Convergence at each individual point.
items.intuition: Each point has its own limit story.
differentia: fixed point x before taking limit

items.label: Uniform convergence
items.definition: Convergence with one global error bound over the whole domain.
items.intuition: The whole graph is captured at once.

items.label: L2 convergence
items.definition: Convergence in mean-square norm.
items.intuition: The average squared error tends to zero; isolated point failures may be invisible.

items.label: Almost everywhere convergence
items.formal_statement: S_N(f)(x)\to f(x)\text{ for all }x\text{ outside a null set}.
items.definition: Pointwise convergence except on a set of measure zero.
items.intuition: The bad points may exist but occupy no measure.

items.label: Distributional convergence
items.formal_statement: T_N\to T\text{ in }\mathcal S^\prime\iff \langle T_N,\varphi\rangle\to\langle T,\varphi\rangle\text{ for all }\varphi\in\mathcal S.
items.definition: Convergence tested against smooth rapidly decreasing functions.
items.intuition: Weak convergence through every smooth measuring device.

items.label: Mean-square convergence of Fourier series
items.statement: Fourier partial sums converge to f in L2 on the circle.
items.formal_statement: If f\in L^2(\mathbb T), then \|S_N(f)-f\|_{L^2(\mathbb T)}\to0.
items.intuition: Partial sums are orthogonal projections onto larger frequency subspaces.
hypotheses: f belongs to L2(T) or an appropriate integrable pre-Hilbert setting completed in L2
items.conclusion: S_N(f) converges to f in L2

items.label: Parseval identity on the circle
items.statement: The L2 energy of a function equals the l2 energy of its Fourier coefficients.
items.intuition: Energy is conserved when changing from time/space coordinates to frequency coordinates.
hypotheses: f in L2(T)
items.conclusion: Fourier coefficient map preserves norm

items.label: Bessel inequality
items.statement: The squared lengths of projections onto an orthonormal family cannot exceed the total squared norm.
items.intuition: You cannot extract more orthogonal energy than the vector contains.
hypotheses: (e_n) is an orthonormal family
f lies in a Hilbert space
items.conclusion: sum of squared coefficients is bounded by norm squared

items.label: Riemann localization principle
items.statement: Pointwise convergence of a Fourier series at a point depends only on the behavior of the function near that point.
items.intuition: Although coefficients see the whole function, high-frequency cancellation makes local behavior decide pointwise convergence.
hypotheses: local hypotheses near the point
items.conclusion: local agreement implies same pointwise convergence behavior

items.label: Dirichlet pointwise convergence theorem
items.statement: Under bounded variation or piecewise smooth-type hypotheses, Fourier series converge at a point to the average of the one-sided limits.
items.intuition: At a jump, oscillations balance the two sides and land at the midpoint.
hypotheses: one-sided limits exist
sufficient local regularity such as bounded variation or piecewise smoothness
items.conclusion: convergence to midpoint of one-sided limits

items.label: Carleson-Hunt theorem
items.statement: Fourier series of Lp functions converge almost everywhere for p>1; Carleson proved the L2 case, Hunt extended to p>1.
items.intuition: Even rough L2 data has almost-everywhere Fourier recovery, but the proof requires deep time-frequency control.
hypotheses: f in Lp(T)
items.conclusion: S_N(f)(x) converges to f(x) for almost every x

items.label: Kolmogorov L1 divergence example
items.statement: There exists an L1 function whose Fourier series diverges almost everywhere.
items.intuition: L1 is too large for raw pointwise Fourier recovery.

items.label: Continuous function with divergent Fourier series
items.statement: There exists a continuous periodic function whose Fourier series diverges at at least one point.
items.intuition: Continuity alone does not tame the Dirichlet kernel oscillations.

items.label: Gibbs phenomenon
items.statement: Near a jump discontinuity, Fourier partial sums exhibit persistent overshoot that does not vanish in height, only in width.
items.intuition: Sharp jumps require infinitely many frequencies; truncation causes ringing near the edge.

items.label: Inner product space
items.definition: A vector space equipped with an inner product.
items.intuition: A space where length, angle, and projection make sense.

items.label: Hilbert space
items.definition: A complete inner product space.
items.intuition: Infinite-dimensional Euclidean geometry where Cauchy sequences have limits.

metadata.source: Bowers-Kalton, An Introductory Course in Functional Analysis (2014)

items.label: L2 space
items.definition: The space of square-integrable functions modulo equality almost everywhere.
items.intuition: Signals with finite energy.

items.label: Lp space
items.definition: The space of p-integrable functions modulo equality almost everywhere.
items.intuition: Function spaces organized by how much size/integrability they allow.

metadata.source: Bowers-Kalton, An Introductory Course in Functional Analysis (2014)

items.label: L1 space
items.definition: The space of integrable functions.
items.intuition: Functions with finite total mass.

items.label: Orthonormal basis
items.definition: A complete orthonormal family in a Hilbert space.
items.intuition: Coordinate axes for an infinite-dimensional space.
differentia: spans densely or every vector has expansion

items.label: Riesz-Fischer theorem
items.statement: The Fourier transform identifies L2(T) isometrically with l2(Z).
items.formal_statement: F:L^2(\mathbb T)\to\ell^2(\mathbb Z),\quad F(f)=(\widehat f(n))_{n\in\mathbb Z} is an isometric isomorphism.
items.intuition: Fourier coefficients are exactly the Hilbert-space coordinates of the function.

metadata.source: Bowers-Kalton, An Introductory Course in Functional Analysis (2014)

hypotheses: f in L2(T)
items.conclusion: L2(T) and l2(Z) are isometrically isomorphic via Fourier coefficients

items.label: Bounded linear operator
items.definition: A linear map between normed spaces that maps the unit ball to a bounded set.
items.intuition: A continuous infinite-dimensional matrix.

metadata.source: Bowers-Kalton, An Introductory Course in Functional Analysis (2014)

items.label: Spectral viewpoint
items.statement: Analyze an operator by decomposing into eigenfunctions or generalized frequencies.
items.intuition: Fourier analysis diagonalizes translation-invariant differential operators.

items.label: Translation operator
items.definition: The operator that shifts a function by h.
items.intuition: Pure frequencies are eigenfunctions of translations.

items.label: Modulation operator
items.definition: Multiplication by a pure frequency.
items.intuition: Shift in frequency space.

items.label: Real line
items.definition: The noncompact one-dimensional Euclidean domain.
items.intuition: Frequencies become continuous because the domain no longer loops.

items.label: Euclidean space R^d
items.definition: d-dimensional real vector space with Euclidean geometry.
items.intuition: Frequency is a vector direction and oscillation rate.

items.label: Schwartz space
items.formal_statement: \mathcal S(\mathbb R^d)=\{f\in C^\infty: \sup_x |x^\alpha\partial^\beta f(x)|<\infty\text{ for all }\alpha,\beta\}.
items.definition: Smooth functions whose derivatives decay faster than any polynomial.
items.intuition: Ideal test functions: smooth, rapidly fading, stable under Fourier transform.

items.label: Function of moderate decrease
items.definition: A continuous function decaying fast enough, typically like O((1+|x|^2)^{-1}) in one dimension, for basic improper Fourier integrals to make sense.
items.intuition: Enough decay to integrate over the infinite line.

items.label: Fourier transform on R^d
items.definition: The transform measuring the continuous-frequency content of f.
items.intuition: A continuum of dot products with plane waves.
differentia: frequency variable lies in dual Euclidean space

items.label: Inverse Fourier transform
items.definition: The reconstruction operator from frequency space to physical space.
items.intuition: Rebuild the function by adding all continuous frequencies.

items.label: Fourier inversion on Schwartz space
items.statement: Schwartz functions are recovered from their Fourier transforms.
items.formal_statement: If f\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R^d), then f=\mathcal F^{-1}(\widehat f).
items.intuition: For rapidly decreasing smooth functions, frequency data is lossless.
hypotheses: f in Schwartz space
items.conclusion: inverse Fourier transform recovers f pointwise

items.label: Plancherel theorem on R^d
items.statement: The Fourier transform preserves L2 norm.
items.intuition: Frequency coordinates preserve energy.
hypotheses: initially f in Schwartz space; extends to L2
items.conclusion: Fourier transform is unitary on L2 after extension

items.label: Convolution theorem for Fourier transforms
items.statement: Fourier transform turns convolution into pointwise multiplication.
items.intuition: Averaging in space becomes filtering in frequency.
hypotheses: f and g in Schwartz space or suitable integrability classes
items.conclusion: Transform of convolution equals product of transforms

items.label: Product-convolution duality
items.statement: Pointwise multiplication in physical space corresponds to convolution in frequency space.
items.intuition: Windowing a signal spreads its frequencies.
items.conclusion: Pointwise multiplication in physical space corresponds to convolution in frequency space.

items.label: Differentiation-multiplier rule
items.statement: Fourier transform turns differentiation into multiplication by a polynomial in frequency.
items.intuition: A pure wave is an eigenfunction of differentiation.
hypotheses: f in Schwartz space or distribution setting
items.conclusion: Derivatives become algebraic frequency multipliers

items.label: Multiplication-differentiation rule
items.statement: Multiplication by position becomes differentiation in frequency.
items.intuition: Localization in space creates frequency variation.
items.conclusion: Multiplication by position becomes differentiation in frequency.

items.label: Gaussian as Fourier eigenfunction
items.definition: The normalized Gaussian is fixed by the Fourier transform.
items.intuition: The most balanced object between space and frequency.

items.label: Radial function
items.definition: A function depending only on distance from the origin.
items.intuition: A function with no preferred direction.

items.label: Fourier transform preserves radiality
items.statement: The Fourier transform of a radial function is radial.
items.intuition: Rotational symmetry survives the passage to frequency space.
hypotheses: f is radial and Fourier transform is defined
items.conclusion: hat f is radial

items.label: Poisson summation formula
items.statement: A lattice sum of a function equals a lattice sum of its Fourier transform.
items.formal_statement: \sum_{n\in\mathbb Z^d} f(n)=\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z^d}\widehat f(n)\quad\text{under suitable hypotheses}.
items.intuition: Periodizing in space samples in frequency.
hypotheses: f sufficiently regular and decaying, e.g. Schwartz
items.conclusion: integer samples of f and hat f have equal sums

items.label: Heisenberg uncertainty principle
items.statement: A nonzero function and its Fourier transform cannot both be arbitrarily concentrated.
items.formal_statement: Variance_x(f)\,Variance_\xi(\widehat f)\ge C_d\|f\|_2^4 under normalized conventions.
items.intuition: A narrow spike needs many frequencies; a narrow spectrum forces spatial spread.
hypotheses: f in suitable dense class such as Schwartz
finite variances
items.conclusion: space-frequency concentration has a positive lower bound

items.label: Test function
items.definition: A smooth function used as a probe for distributions; in tempered distribution theory the tests are Schwartz functions.
items.intuition: A measuring device: smooth, controlled, and easy to differentiate.

items.label: Distribution
items.definition: A continuous linear functional on a test-function space.
items.intuition: A generalized function known only by its action on probes.

metadata.source: standard distribution theory / Schwartz tempered distributions

differentia: acts on smooth compactly supported test functions

items.label: Tempered distribution
items.formal_statement: \mathcal S^\prime(\mathbb R^d)=\{\text{continuous linear functionals on }\mathcal S(\mathbb R^d)\}.
items.definition: A distribution that grows at most polynomially and can be Fourier transformed.
items.intuition: A rough or growing object still compatible with Schwartz probes.

metadata.source: standard distribution theory / Schwartz tempered distributions

items.label: Dirac delta
items.definition: The point mass distribution at a.
items.intuition: All mass concentrated at one point; not a classical function.

metadata.source: standard distribution theory / Schwartz tempered distributions

items.label: Weak derivative
items.definition: Derivative defined by moving derivatives onto test functions.
items.intuition: Differentiate rough objects by integration by parts without boundary terms.

metadata.source: standard distribution theory / Schwartz tempered distributions

differentia: defined by duality against test functions

items.label: Fourier transform of tempered distributions
items.definition: The Fourier transform extended to tempered distributions by duality.
items.intuition: Transform rough objects by transforming the smooth probe.

metadata.source: standard distribution theory / Schwartz tempered distributions

differentia: uses action on transformed test functions

items.label: Sobolev space
items.definition: A function/distribution space measuring differentiability by frequency weights.
items.intuition: Smoothness means high-frequency coefficients decay fast enough.

items.label: Fundamental solution
items.definition: A distribution solving a linear PDE with a point source.
items.intuition: The response of an operator to an impulse.

items.label: Locally integrable function as distribution
items.definition: Embedding ordinary functions into distributions by integration against tests.
items.intuition: Functions are distributions whose measurements are weighted averages.

items.label: Harmonic analysis
items.definition: The broad field studying functions, representations, and operators through oscillation, symmetry, and averaging.
items.intuition: Fourier analysis extended from explicit waves to groups, kernels, singular operators, and scale decompositions.

items.label: Locally compact abelian group
items.definition: A topological abelian group that is locally compact.
items.intuition: The natural setting where Haar measure and characters support Fourier analysis.

items.label: Haar measure
items.definition: A translation-invariant measure on a locally compact group.
items.intuition: The measure that makes averaging respect group symmetry.

metadata.source: Bowers-Kalton, An Introductory Course in Functional Analysis (2014)

items.label: Pontryagin duality
items.statement: Locally compact abelian groups are recovered from their character groups under the double dual.
items.intuition: Frequencies of frequencies reconstruct the original group.
hypotheses: G locally compact abelian
items.conclusion: canonical map G to double dual is an isomorphism

items.label: Fourier multiplier
items.definition: An operator acting by multiplying each frequency by a symbol.
items.intuition: A frequency-dependent filter.

items.label: Hilbert transform
items.definition: A singular integral operator and Fourier multiplier with symbol -i sign(xi).
items.intuition: A ninety-degree phase shifter for real signals and a prototype singular integral.

items.label: Riesz transforms
items.definition: Higher-dimensional analogues of the Hilbert transform.
items.intuition: Directional phase-gradient operators.

items.label: Hardy-Littlewood maximal operator
items.definition: The supremum over local averages of |f|.
items.intuition: A microscope scanning every scale around x.

items.label: Calderon-Zygmund singular integral operator
items.definition: A singular integral operator with a kernel satisfying size and smoothness estimates.
items.intuition: Controlled nonlocal cancellation; the safe way to integrate a singular kernel.

items.label: Littlewood-Paley decomposition
items.definition: A decomposition of a function into dyadic frequency bands.
items.intuition: Analyze a signal scale by scale, like a frequency microscope.

items.label: Fourier restriction problem
items.statement: Study when the Fourier transform of an Lp function has meaningful restriction to lower-dimensional curved surfaces.
items.intuition: Curvature creates cancellation that may allow traces in frequency space.

items.label: Fourier method for PDEs
items.statement: Apply the Fourier transform to convert differential operators into algebraic multipliers or ODEs in time.
items.intuition: Differentiation becomes multiplication, so a PDE becomes frequency-wise scalar equations.

items.label: Wave equation
items.definition: A PDE modeling propagation of waves.
items.intuition: Initial displacement and velocity split into moving oscillations.

items.label: Heat equation
items.definition: A PDE modeling diffusion.
items.intuition: High frequencies decay rapidly, so heat flow smooths.

items.label: Laplace equation
items.definition: The steady-state equation for harmonic functions.
items.intuition: No sources: each value balances neighboring values.

items.label: Harmonic function
items.definition: A twice differentiable function satisfying Laplace equation.
items.intuition: A function whose value is locally balanced like equilibrium temperature.

items.label: Dirichlet problem in disk
items.statement: Find a harmonic function in the unit disk with prescribed boundary values.
items.intuition: Boundary temperature determines equilibrium inside via Poisson averaging.

items.label: Heat kernel
items.definition: The fundamental smoothing kernel for the heat equation.
items.intuition: A Gaussian blur whose width grows with time.

items.label: Poisson kernel in upper half-plane
items.definition: The harmonic extension kernel for the upper half-plane.
items.intuition: Boundary averaging seen from height y.

items.label: Radon transform
items.definition: The transform integrating a function over hyperplanes.
items.intuition: X-ray data: line/plane shadows from all directions.

items.label: Fourier slice theorem
items.statement: The one-dimensional Fourier transform of Radon data equals a slice of the full Fourier transform.
items.intuition: Each X-ray direction reveals a radial line in frequency space.
hypotheses: f in Schwartz space or suitable tomography class
items.conclusion: Radon transform data gives Fourier transform on lines

items.label: Plane wave
items.definition: A wave constant on planes perpendicular to a direction and traveling along that direction.
items.intuition: A flat wavefront moving through space.

items.label: Computed tomography
items.statement: Reconstruct an internal density from many X-ray projections using Radon and Fourier methods.
items.intuition: Collect shadows from enough angles and invert them in frequency space.

items.label: Fourier methods in Black-Scholes theory
items.statement: Fourier transform methods can solve or represent solutions of diffusion-type financial PDEs.
items.intuition: Option pricing equations resemble heat equations after changes of variables.

items.label: Finite abelian group
items.definition: A finite group with commutative operation.
items.intuition: A finite symmetry space where sums replace integrals.

items.label: Cyclic group Z/NZ
items.definition: Integers modulo N under addition.
items.intuition: A discrete circle with N equally spaced points.

items.label: Discrete Fourier transform
items.definition: Fourier transform for data on a finite cyclic group.
items.intuition: Measure alignment of a finite signal with N discrete waves.

items.label: Finite Fourier inversion
items.statement: The DFT is invertible.
items.intuition: The N finite waves form a complete coordinate system for N data points.
hypotheses: x is a function on Z/NZ
items.conclusion: x is reconstructed from its DFT

items.label: Finite Plancherel identity
items.statement: DFT preserves energy up to normalization.
items.intuition: Finite frequency coordinates rotate the data vector without losing length.
items.conclusion: DFT preserves energy up to normalization.

items.label: Fast Fourier transform
items.definition: An algorithm computing the DFT using divide-and-conquer symmetries, typically in O(N log N) operations.
items.intuition: Split even and odd samples so repeated structure avoids redundant work.

items.label: Sampling theorem
items.statement: Band-limited signals can be reconstructed from sufficiently dense samples.
items.intuition: If no hidden high frequencies exist, discrete samples capture all information.
hypotheses: signal is band-limited
sample rate exceeds twice maximum frequency under standard normalization
items.conclusion: exact reconstruction from samples is possible

items.label: Aliasing
items.statement: Different continuous frequencies can become indistinguishable after sampling.
items.intuition: Sampling wraps frequency space modulo the sampling rate.

items.label: Filtering
items.statement: Modify selected frequencies to smooth, denoise, enhance, or isolate signal components.
items.intuition: Convolution in time or space is multiplication in frequency.

items.label: Windowing and spectral leakage
items.statement: Multiplying a signal by a finite window convolves its spectrum with the window spectrum, spreading frequency energy.
items.intuition: Cutting a signal in time blurs its frequency.

items.label: Power spectrum
items.definition: The squared magnitude of Fourier coefficients or transform values, interpreted as frequency energy distribution.
items.intuition: How much energy lives at each frequency.

items.label: Sawtooth function
items.definition: A periodic function with a jump discontinuity at the boundary.
items.intuition: A ramp reset each period; its Fourier coefficients decay like 1/n.

items.label: Square wave
items.definition: A periodic signal alternating between two constant values.
items.intuition: A pure discontinuous switching signal requiring infinitely many odd harmonics.

items.label: Plucked string
items.definition: A triangular initial displacement of a fixed string.
items.intuition: Sharp corners create harmonic content with decaying amplitudes.

items.label: White noise
items.definition: An idealized generalized stochastic signal with flat power spectrum.
items.intuition: Equal frequency power at every scale; too rough to be an ordinary function.

items.label: Dirac delta is not a classical function
items.statement: No ordinary locally integrable function has the exact point-evaluation action of the Dirac delta.
items.intuition: A true point mass has zero width but nonzero total mass.

items.label: Aliasing example
items.statement: Two sinusoids whose frequencies differ by an integer multiple of the sampling rate have identical samples.
items.intuition: Sampling folds the frequency axis into a discrete circle.

items.label: Smooth compactly supported bump
items.definition: A C-infinity function that is zero outside a compact interval but nonzero inside.
items.intuition: A perfectly smooth local probe.

items.label: High-frequency sine wave
items.definition: A pure oscillation with frequency N.
items.intuition: As N grows, oscillations cancel in averages but not pointwise.

items.label: Weak convergence by oscillation
items.statement: Highly oscillatory functions may converge weakly to zero even when their pointwise size does not vanish.
items.intuition: Positive and negative waves cancel under smooth measurement.

items.label: Proof sketch of Fejer theorem
items.strategy: Use sigma_N(f)=f*F_N and verify F_N is a good positive approximate identity; split the convolution error into near and far arcs.

items.label: Fejer proof step: identify kernel
items.claim: The Cesaro mean sigma_N(f) equals convolution f*F_N.
items.justification: Average the identities S_k(f)=f*D_k and collect the averaged kernel.

items.label: Fejer proof step: positivity and concentration
items.claim: The Fejer kernels form a good kernel family.
items.justification: Closed-form positivity, normalized integral, and decay away from zero verify the approximate-identity hypotheses.

items.label: Fejer proof step: near/far split
items.claim: The convolution error tends uniformly to zero for continuous f.
items.justification: Uniform continuity controls nearby values; the good-kernel tail controls far values.

items.label: Proof sketch of Plancherel theorem
items.strategy: Use Fourier inversion and convolution identities on Schwartz functions, then extend by density to L2.

items.label: Plancherel proof step: build autocorrelation
items.claim: For h=f*tilde f, the Fourier transform of h is |hat f|^2.
items.justification: Apply the convolution theorem and reflection/conjugation.

items.label: Plancherel proof step: apply inversion at zero
items.claim: The integral of |hat f|^2 equals h(0)=int |f|^2.
items.justification: Fourier inversion applied to h at x=0.

items.label: Plancherel proof step: extend to L2
items.claim: The transform extends uniquely to a unitary operator on L2.
items.justification: Schwartz functions are dense in L2 and the norm identity makes the transform continuous.

items.label: Proof sketch of Fourier slice theorem
items.strategy: Parametrize hyperplanes perpendicular to gamma, then take the one-dimensional Fourier transform in the offset variable.
critical_assumptions: Fubini/change of variables justified by rapid decay

items.label: Slice theorem proof step: parametrize planes
items.claim: A point x can be written as t gamma plus a vector u perpendicular to gamma.
items.justification: Decompose Euclidean space into the gamma direction and its orthogonal plane.

items.label: Slice theorem proof step: identify full transform
items.claim: The transformed Radon data equals hat f(s gamma).
items.justification: After substituting x=t gamma+u, the exponential is e^{-2pi i s gamma dot x}.

items.label: Continuous periodic function
items.definition: A continuous function on the circle.
items.intuition: A looped graph with no jumps.

items.label: Piecewise smooth function
items.definition: A function smooth except at finitely or countably many controlled breakpoints.
items.intuition: Smooth arcs joined by possible corners or jumps.

items.label: Ordinary function
items.definition: A pointwise-defined function, as opposed to a generalized function/distribution.
items.intuition: Something with values at points rather than only test-function measurements.

items.label: Sampled signal
items.definition: A sequence of values obtained from a continuous signal at discrete times or positions.
items.intuition: A continuous wave observed through a grid.

items.label: Unique continuous frequency recovery
items.statement: Continuous frequencies are recoverable from samples only under restrictions such as band-limitation and adequate sampling rate.
items.intuition: Without prior bounds, samples cannot distinguish all continuous waves.

items.label: Wave equation initial data
items.definition: The initial displacement and velocity used to determine a wave equation solution.
items.intuition: The starting shape and push of the string or field.

items.label: Change of variables
items.statement: Transform an integral by replacing variables and accounting for the Jacobian.
items.intuition: Choose coordinates that reveal the geometry of the integral.

items.label: Oscillatory cancellation
items.statement: Use rapid sign/phase changes to make integrals small.
items.intuition: Many wave crests and troughs cancel when averaged.

items.label: Density argument
items.statement: Prove a result on a dense simple class, then extend by approximation.
items.intuition: Solve it where computations are clean, then pass to limits.

items.label: Gaussian regularization
items.statement: Insert Gaussian factors or heat kernels to justify limiting Fourier operations.
items.intuition: Blur first, prove safely, then remove the blur.

items.label: Dirichlet kernel estimates
items.statement: Analyze partial sums through oscillatory estimates for D_N.
items.intuition: Control the raw summation kernel despite its bad averaging behavior.

items.label: Character orthogonality
items.statement: Distinct characters of a finite abelian group are orthogonal.
items.intuition: Different finite frequencies cancel over the full group.
hypotheses: G finite abelian
chi and psi characters
items.conclusion: characters are orthonormal after normalization

items.label: Time-frequency analysis
items.statement: Analyze functions using simultaneous localization in time/space and frequency.
items.intuition: Track where frequencies occur, not only which frequencies exist.

edges.label: integer frequencies are characters on the circle
kolmogorov_l1_divergence_counterexample shows_necessity_of carleson_hunt_theorem
edges.notes: Shows p>1 cannot be replaced by p=1 in this form.

edges.label: lp_space generalizes l2_space
edges.notes: Lp family includes L2 as the Hilbert case.

edges.label: convolution_theorem_transform generalizes convolution_circle
edges.notes: Euclidean convolution theorem is analogous to circle convolution/Fourier coefficient product rules.

edges.label: ps_fejer_kernel_identity is a step in proof_fejer_theorem
ps_fejer_good_kernel is a step in proof_fejer_theorem
ps_fejer_near_far_split is a step in proof_fejer_theorem
ps_plancherel_convolution is a step in proof_plancherel_Rd
ps_plancherel_inversion_at_zero is a step in proof_plancherel_Rd
ps_plancherel_density_extension is a step in proof_plancherel_Rd
ps_slice_parametrize is a step in proof_fourier_slice
ps_slice_change_variables is a step in proof_fourier_slice

source_references.label: Stein & Shakarchi, Fourier Analysis: An Introduction
source_references.notes: Primary backbone for Fourier series, transforms, PDE links, Radon transform, finite Fourier analysis.

MIT notes on tempered distributions and Fourier transform
Stanford EE261 Fourier Transform and Applications

concept_map.description: Conceptual dependency and relationship view of Fourier analysis.
concept_map.example_traversal: Start from a root item and traverse requires/prerequisite_for edges by depth.

proof_graph.description: Proof-oriented view linking proofs, proof steps, methods, and results.
proof_graph.example_traversal: From theorem, find incoming proves edges, then traverse proof steps and uses edges.

example_counterexample_map.description: Examples and counterexamples attached to concepts, hypotheses, and theorems.
example_counterexample_map.example_traversal: From a concept or theorem, follow example and counterexample edges.

query_model.description: Query by stable item id; traverse directed typed edges while preserving dependency_class. Do not collapse parallel edges.

traversal_primitives.outgoing: Follow graph.edges whose source equals the current item id.
traversal_primitives.incoming: Follow graph.edges whose target equals the current item id.
traversal_primitives.both: Follow both incoming and outgoing edges while preserving direction in returned paths.

dependency_semantics.definitional_dependency: Needed to state or define the item.
dependency_semantics.assumption_dependency: Hypotheses needed for the statement to hold.
dependency_semantics.construction_dependency: Data or prior construction from which an object is built.
dependency_semantics.notation_dependency: Notation required to parse the item.
dependency_semantics.pedagogical_dependency: Useful learning order or explanatory relation.
